data: The Most Advanced Model, the Deepest Affordability Crisis

Over 19,000 terrestrial fiber cuts in early 2025 made satellite connectivity a strategic necessity. Nigeria has since adopted the most advanced sovereignty protections of any studied country — yet hardware costs 4.5 months of minimum wage.
5 providers authorized · 2 operational · 128 million Nigerians unconnected
Most advanced sovereignty model of all studied countries — yet hardware costs 4.5 months of minimum wage, excluding the majority
Nigeria transitioned in 2026 from a Starlink-only model to a multi-constellation policy to ensure market resilience and end single-provider dominance.
Nigeria mandates that LEO providers land data at local gateways and form local partnerships to ensure digital sovereignty.
Ending Starlink's single-provider dominance through the issuance of multiple permits has created a diverse and competitive ecosystem.
A key gap is the absence of a local technical presence requirement, leaving rural users without physical support for hardware failures.

Nigeria's LEO story is defined by crisis and inequality. Over 19,000 terrestrial fiber cuts in early 2025 made satellite connectivity a strategic necessity rather than a planned transition. In 2026, the market transitioned from a Starlink monopoly toward a multi-constellation framework with five authorized providers (two operational — Starlink and Eutelsat OneWeb — and three more holding 7-year landing permits). Nigeria has also adopted the most advanced sovereignty protections of any studied country — mandating local data gateway landing, local partnerships, and a biometric NIN registration requirement for all Starlink users. Yet the hardware barrier remains extreme: at ₦318,000, a Starlink Mini kit costs 4.5 months of minimum-wage income. With 128 million Nigerians still unconnected and Amazon Leo targeting 400 Mbps to disrupt pricing, Nigeria simultaneously represents the best governance model and the deepest affordability crisis.

7-year landing-permit model; mandates local data gateway and local partnerships. All Starlink users required to link terminals to national ID (NIN) as of December 31, 2025. Most advanced regulatory framework of studied countries.

3 of 7 governance indicators met — the strongest scorecard among studied countries. A mandatory cybersecurity audit is not yet an active requirement; the pending National Digital Economy and E-Governance Bill would empower NITDA to audit satellite providers in future.
Hardware kit (₦318,000) costs 4.5 months of minimum wage — 93.8% of average monthly salary
128 million Nigerians remain unconnected — LEO reaches only those who can afford the hardware
LEO became a strategic necessity due to infrastructure failure (19,000+ fiber cuts) — not a planned transition
Satellite traffic can bypass local monitoring towers — intelligence and data residency gaps
Subsidized hardware programme linked to Universal Service Fund
Enforce and monitor local data gateway compliance
Expand multi-constellation competition to drive hardware price reduction
Address intelligence gap from satellite traffic bypassing local towers
Who controls satellite internet? We’re tracking who profits from connectivity in space and who gets left behind.

LEO satellite governance, Starlink regulation, digital sovereignty, satellite internet policy, orbital commons, satellite market concentration, connectivity divide, spectrum equity, ITU WRC-27, satellite licensing transparency
